summary:
Arterial carbon dioxide concentration primarily drives respiratory center stimulation via its effects on chemoreceptors. This mechanism supersedes other influences when considering direct excitatory impact on breathing control centers. To summarize Agent2’s reasoning into two concise sentences focusing only on critical aspects related to identifying what stimulates the respiratory center most effectively based on provided information:

1) Increased arterial carbon dioxide acts dominantly upon chemoreceptors, enhancing their signaling which strongly activates the respiratory center more than alternative physiological triggers.
   
2) While neural circuitry establishes baseline patterns, it does not match the potent stimulus exerted directly from elevated blood \(CO_2\) levels detected at chemosensitive receptors influencing breath rate and depth intensively under varying conditions. Summary: Elevated arterial carbon dioxide concentration mainly drives respiratory center activation through enhanced signals received by chemoreceptors. Other potential influencers are less effective compared to high \(CO_2\) in robustly exciting the respiratory system according to available data.
